prompt:
You are a clinical bibliotherapist. Based on the therapeutic goal, family context, and academic research papers below, recommend 4-6 real, published books that would be most helpful.

## Therapeutic Goal
Goal: Help my 7-year-old regulate anger outbursts
Description: Leo has 2-4 explosive meltdowns per week at home, mostly triggered by transitions and homework. We want evidence-based strategies for co-regulation and building his emotional vocabulary.

## Family Context
### Person Profile
**Leo** | Age: 7 | Relationship: son | Bio: Bright, sensitive, strong-willed. Reads above grade level. Struggles with transitions and perceived unfairness.
### Known Issues (2)
- **Homework meltdowns** [moderate/behavior]: Refuses and escalates to yelling and throwing supplies when stuck on math.
- **Transition resistance** [moderate/behavior]: Slow to switch from play to dinner/bed; often ends in tears.

## Research Papers (for grounding)
[1] "Collaborative Problem Solving reduces aggressive behavior in school-age children"
  Abstract: RCT (n=134) comparing CPS to standard parent training. CPS produced larger reductions in oppositional behavior at 6 months.
  Key findings: CPS outperforms reward-based approaches for inflexible-explosive kids; parent stress also drops; gains maintained at 12mo
  Techniques: Plan B conversations; empathy step; defining the problem; brainstorming solutions
  Evidence: randomized controlled trial

[2] "Interpersonal neurobiology and the developing brain: co-regulation before self-regulation"
  Abstract: Review of affective-neuroscience evidence that children's prefrontal control develops through repeated co-regulation experiences with attuned caregivers.
  Key findings: Self-regulation is built on scaffolding; name-to-tame reduces limbic arousal; window of tolerance widens with secure base
  Techniques: Name it to tame it; connect before correct; hand model of the brain
  Evidence: systematic review

[3] "Window of tolerance and polyvagal-informed parenting interventions"
  Abstract: Observational study of 80 parent-child dyads showing polyvagal-informed scripts reduced escalation time by 40%.
  Key findings: Co-regulation via prosody and posture; ventral vagal cues; staying below the child's threshold
  Techniques: Soft eyes and voice; body posture matching; regulation before reasoning
  Evidence: observational

[4] "Executive function scaffolding for emotionally intense children"
  Abstract: Meta-analysis of 22 studies on EF-supporting parental strategies.
  Key findings: Visual schedules reduce transition meltdowns; flexible thinking games build tolerance; lagging skills framing
  Techniques: Visual timers; first-then boards; flexibility practice games
  Evidence: meta-analysis

## Instructions
Recommend 4-6 books that:
- Are REAL, well-known published books (do NOT invent titles)
- Cover diverse categories (mix of parenting guides, therapy workbooks, psychology, child development)
- Are directly relevant to the therapeutic goal and research findings
- Would be accessible and practical for a parent or caregiver
- Range from introductory to advanced where appropriate

For each book provide: title (exact), authors (array of full names), year (int, optional), isbn (string, optional), description (brief content summary), whyRecommended (personalized rationale linking to the goal, research, and family context), category (one of: parenting, therapy, self-help, child development, education, psychology, neuroscience).

Respond with a JSON object of the shape {"books": [ {...}, ... ]}. The top-level key MUST be "books" and its value MUST be an array.

goal_title: Support my 15-year-old daughter with generalized anxiety
prompt:
You are a clinical bibliotherapist. Based on the therapeutic goal, family context, and academic research papers below, recommend 4-6 real, published books that would be most helpful.

## Therapeutic Goal
Goal: Support my 15-year-old daughter with generalized anxiety
Description: Mia has persistent worry, sleep-onset insomnia, and school-avoidance mornings. Therapist recommended CBT; we want companion books she can engage with and resources for parents.

## Family Context
### Person Profile
**Mia** | Age: 15 | Relationship: daughter | Bio: High-achieving, perfectionist, socially anxious. Recently started 10th grade; grades are slipping as she avoids classes that cause stomach aches.
### Known Issues (2)
- **Morning school avoidance** [moderate/anxiety]: Somatic complaints and panic around leaving the house on school days.
- **Rumination at bedtime** [moderate/anxiety]: Can't fall asleep, replays social interactions and hypothetical failures.

## Research Papers (for grounding)
[1] "Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for adolescent anxiety disorders: a meta-analysis"
  Abstract: 47 RCTs, n=3,215. CBT shows large effect on anxiety symptoms (Hedges' g = 0.77) with strongest gains when exposure is included.
  Key findings: Exposure hierarchies outperform relaxation-only; parent involvement helps pre-teens more than teens; relapse prevention matters
  Techniques: Thought records; graded exposure; cognitive restructuring; behavioral experiments
  Evidence: meta-analysis

[2] "Worry postponement and metacognitive therapy for GAD in adolescents"
  Abstract: Single-case series (n=18) showing metacognitive techniques reduce uncontrollability of worry.
  Key findings: Targeting beliefs about worry outperforms content-level disputation; worry time boxing; detached mindfulness
  Techniques: Worry postponement; attention training; metacognitive delusion challenges
  Evidence: case series

[3] "Acceptance and Commitment Therapy for anxious youth"
  Abstract: RCT of ACT-for-teens vs waitlist (n=92). Large reductions in experiential avoidance and values-based action.
  Key findings: Psychological flexibility mediates outcome; values work drives engagement; defusion reduces fusion with catastrophic thoughts
  Techniques: Cognitive defusion; values clarification; mindful action; committed action
  Evidence: randomized controlled trial

[4] "Sleep-onset insomnia in anxious adolescents"
  Abstract: Longitudinal study showing pre-sleep arousal predicts anxiety relapse.
  Key findings: Stimulus control + cognitive restructuring; wind-down routines; limiting screen exposure
  Techniques: Sleep restriction; bedtime cognitive worry-dump; stimulus control
  Evidence: longitudinal cohort

## Instructions
Recommend 4-6 books that:
- Are REAL, well-known published books (do NOT invent titles)
- Cover diverse categories (mix of parenting guides, therapy workbooks, psychology, child development)
- Are directly relevant to the therapeutic goal and research findings
- Would be accessible and practical for a parent or caregiver
- Range from introductory to advanced where appropriate

For each book provide: title (exact), authors (array of full names), year (int, optional), isbn (string, optional), description (brief content summary), whyRecommended (personalized rationale linking to the goal, research, and family context), category (one of: parenting, therapy, self-help, child development, education, psychology, neuroscience).

Respond with a JSON object of the shape {"books": [ {...}, ... ]}. The top-level key MUST be "books" and its value MUST be an array.

goal_title: Build sustainable focus habits for my adult ADHD
prompt:
You are a clinical bibliotherapist. Based on the therapeutic goal, family context, and academic research papers below, recommend 4-6 real, published books that would be most helpful.

## Therapeutic Goal
Goal: Build sustainable focus habits for my adult ADHD
Description: I'm 34, diagnosed with combined-type ADHD two years ago. Medication helps but I still struggle with task initiation, time blindness, and rejection sensitivity. Looking for practical habit systems grounded in evidence.

## Family Context
### Person Profile
**Alex** | Age: 34 | Relationship: self | Bio: Software engineer, married, two small kids. Diagnosed late; ashamed about the diagnosis. History of burnout from over-committing.
### Known Issues (2)
- **Task initiation paralysis** [severe/executive-function]: Spends hours on phone before starting work; avoids anything that feels boring.
- **Rejection sensitive dysphoria** [moderate/emotion]: Disproportionate distress after mild feedback; days of rumination.

## Research Papers (for grounding)
[1] "Implementation intentions in adult ADHD: an experimental trial"
  Abstract: RCT (n=121) comparing if-then plans to generic goal-setting. If-then conditions produced larger gains on task-initiation latency.
  Key findings: Pairing triggers to actions bypasses executive lag; context-dependent cues work better than time-based; small wins stack
  Techniques: If-then plans; habit stacking; environmental design; friction reduction
  Evidence: randomized controlled trial

[2] "Cognitive-behavioral therapy for adult ADHD: 10-year evidence review"
  Abstract: Review of 31 trials of CBT adjuncts to medication for adult ADHD.
  Key findings: CBT + meds outperforms meds alone for functional outcomes; core skills: planning, time-mgmt, distractibility, procrastination
  Techniques: Externalized planning; time estimation; chunking; behavior-activation-style scheduling
  Evidence: systematic review

[3] "Rejection sensitivity in ADHD and emotion regulation strategies"
  Abstract: Cross-sectional survey (n=604) confirming elevated RSD in ADHD adults and identifying mindfulness + self-compassion as buffers.
  Key findings: RSD not an official DSM feature but highly reported; mindfulness reduces reactivity; self-compassion disrupts shame spiral
  Techniques: Defusion; self-compassion practice; urge surfing; values anchoring
  Evidence: cross-sectional survey

[4] "Atomic behavior change and habit formation in neurodivergent adults"
  Abstract: Observational study, n=212, of adult ADHD participants using structured habit programs.
  Key findings: 2-minute rule reduces initiation friction; habit loop (cue-routine-reward); making it obvious / easy / satisfying
  Techniques: Cue design; rewarding micro-habits; environmental friction
  Evidence: observational

## Instructions
Recommend 4-6 books that:
- Are REAL, well-known published books (do NOT invent titles)
- Cover diverse categories (mix of parenting guides, therapy workbooks, psychology, child development)
- Are directly relevant to the therapeutic goal and research findings
- Would be accessible and practical for a parent or caregiver
- Range from introductory to advanced where appropriate

For each book provide: title (exact), authors (array of full names), year (int, optional), isbn (string, optional), description (brief content summary), whyRecommended (personalized rationale linking to the goal, research, and family context), category (one of: parenting, therapy, self-help, child development, education, psychology, neuroscience).

Respond with a JSON object of the shape {"books": [ {...}, ... ]}. The top-level key MUST be "books" and its value MUST be an array.

goal_title: Repair communication patterns with my spouse after two years of distance
prompt:
You are a clinical bibliotherapist. Based on the therapeutic goal, family context, and academic research papers below, recommend 4-6 real, published books that would be most helpful.

## Therapeutic Goal
Goal: Repair communication patterns with my spouse after two years of distance
Description: We've drifted into a pursue-withdraw loop; most conversations end in either stonewalling or contempt. Interested in evidence-based couples frameworks we can read together.

## Family Context
### Person Profile
**Priya** | Age: 41 | Relationship: self | Bio: Married 12 years, two kids (9 and 6). Trying to avoid separation; both partners open to working on it.
### Known Issues (2)
- **Pursue-withdraw dynamic** [severe/relational]: She approaches, he retreats; escalation follows.
- **Resentment buildup** [moderate/emotion]: Running tally of unspoken grievances; affecting physical intimacy.

## Research Papers (for grounding)
[1] "Emotionally Focused Therapy for couples: 30-year meta-analysis"
  Abstract: Pooled n=1,500 couples across 31 RCTs. 70-75% of couples move from distress to recovery; attachment-based framework.
  Key findings: Negative interaction cycles are the problem, not the content; accessibility-responsiveness-engagement (ARE); attachment injuries require structured repair
  Techniques: Identifying the cycle; de-escalating; restructuring bonds; creating new bonding events
  Evidence: meta-analysis

[2] "The four horsemen and predictive power of contempt: longitudinal marital research"
  Abstract: 40-year longitudinal lab studies predicting divorce with ~93% accuracy.
  Key findings: Contempt is the #1 predictor; 5-to-1 positive-to-negative ratio in healthy couples; repair attempts must be received
  Techniques: Antidotes to four horsemen; soft start-ups; repair attempts; love maps
  Evidence: longitudinal

[3] "Non-violent communication in intimate partners"
  Abstract: Pragmatic trial (n=94 couples) evaluating NVC training.
  Key findings: Observation-without-evaluation reduces defensiveness; need-based language; request vs demand
  Techniques: Observations; feelings; needs; requests
  Evidence: pragmatic trial

[4] "Differentiation of self in marital longevity"
  Abstract: Bowenian family systems research connecting differentiation to marital satisfaction.
  Key findings: Holding on to self while staying connected; lower reactivity predicts higher satisfaction; intergenerational patterns recur
  Techniques: Self-soothing under pressure; non-reactive stance; identifying emotional triggers
  Evidence: cross-sectional

## Instructions
Recommend 4-6 books that:
- Are REAL, well-known published books (do NOT invent titles)
- Cover diverse categories (mix of parenting guides, therapy workbooks, psychology, child development)
- Are directly relevant to the therapeutic goal and research findings
- Would be accessible and practical for a parent or caregiver
- Range from introductory to advanced where appropriate

For each book provide: title (exact), authors (array of full names), year (int, optional), isbn (string, optional), description (brief content summary), whyRecommended (personalized rationale linking to the goal, research, and family context), category (one of: parenting, therapy, self-help, child development, education, psychology, neuroscience).

Respond with a JSON object of the shape {"books": [ {...}, ... ]}. The top-level key MUST be "books" and its value MUST be an array.

goal_title: Navigate postpartum depression four months after my first baby
prompt:
You are a clinical bibliotherapist. Based on the therapeutic goal, family context, and academic research papers below, recommend 4-6 real, published books that would be most helpful.

## Therapeutic Goal
Goal: Navigate postpartum depression four months after my first baby
Description: I'm 31, four months postpartum. Screening put me in moderate PPD range; starting therapy. Looking for books that normalize this experience and offer evidence-based strategies I can apply in micro-moments with a newborn.

## Family Context
### Person Profile
**Sarah** | Age: 31 | Relationship: self | Bio: First-time mom. Breastfeeding. Partner supportive but works long hours. Feeling disconnected from baby and guilty about it.
### Known Issues (2)
- **Intrusive thoughts** [moderate/anxiety]: Catastrophic thoughts about harm coming to the baby; frightened by them.
- **Emotional flatness** [moderate/mood]: Difficulty feeling joy or connection; going through the motions.

## Research Papers (for grounding)
[1] "Perinatal depression: neuroscience, hormones, and evidence-based treatment"
  Abstract: Review of 120 studies on biological and psychosocial drivers of PPD.
  Key findings: Sleep deprivation amplifies risk; CBT and IPT equally effective; SSRIs safe with breastfeeding; support groups add adherence
  Techniques: Sleep protection protocols; behavioral activation; interpersonal therapy; cognitive restructuring
  Evidence: systematic review

[2] "Intrusive thoughts in postpartum women: prevalence, triggers, and interventions"
  Abstract: Cross-sectional study (n=930). 57% of new mothers report intrusive thoughts; not correlated with harm risk but highly distressing.
  Key findings: Normalizing is therapeutic; thought-action fusion drives distress; ERP-informed strategies help
  Techniques: Psychoeducation; thought defusion; exposure-and-response-prevention-lite
  Evidence: cross-sectional

[3] "Self-compassion as mediator in postpartum adjustment"
  Abstract: Longitudinal cohort (n=340) from pregnancy through 12 months postpartum.
  Key findings: Self-compassion buffers against depression and shame; common humanity component reduces isolation; mindful self-compassion protocols
  Techniques: Loving-kindness; compassionate letter; self-compassion break
  Evidence: longitudinal

[4] "Behavioral activation in mothers with limited time windows"
  Abstract: Pilot trial (n=48) of BA adapted for early parenting.
  Key findings: Small meaningful activities outperform motivation-based approaches; values ladder; pleasure/mastery tracking
  Techniques: Values identification; activity scheduling; mastery tracking
  Evidence: pilot trial

## Instructions
Recommend 4-6 books that:
- Are REAL, well-known published books (do NOT invent titles)
- Cover diverse categories (mix of parenting guides, therapy workbooks, psychology, child development)
- Are directly relevant to the therapeutic goal and research findings
- Would be accessible and practical for a parent or caregiver
- Range from introductory to advanced where appropriate

Respond with a JSON object of the shape {"books": [ {...}, ... ]}. The top-level key MUST be "books" and its value MUST be an array.

You are a clinical bibliotherapist. Based on the therapeutic goal, family context, and academic research papers below, recommend 4-6 real, published books that would be most helpful.

## Therapeutic Goal
Goal: Resolve 18-month-old toddler sleep onset difficulties
Description: Nora takes 45-90 min to fall asleep and wakes 3-4x per night. We're exhausted. Looking for gentle, attachment-informed approaches rather than full extinction crying.

## Family Context
### Person Profile
**Nora** | Age: 1 | Relationship: daughter | Bio: Breastfed to sleep until 14 months. Active, highly social, teething often. Slow-to-warm temperament.
### Known Issues (1)
- **Sleep onset resistance** [moderate/sleep]: Needs contact to fall asleep; protests bedtime even when clearly tired.

## Research Papers (for grounding)
[1] "Behavioral and parent-presence interventions for infant sleep"
  Abstract: Cochrane-style review of 28 trials including extinction, chair method, and parent-presence protocols.
  Key findings: All structured methods work; gradual approaches don't harm attachment; parent stress and depression also drop; sleep associations are the mechanism
  Techniques: Fading; chair method; check-and-console; sleep associations
  Evidence: systematic review

[2] "Circadian biology in infants and toddlers"
  Abstract: Review of chronobiology research for early childhood.
  Key findings: Wake windows matter more than clock time; bright-light exposure anchors rhythm; consistent wake time > consistent bedtime
  Techniques: Wake window matching; morning light; dim-light transitions
  Evidence: narrative review

[3] "Attachment-sensitive approaches to infant sleep"
  Abstract: Mixed-methods study (n=112) of attachment-informed sleep coaching.
  Key findings: Responsiveness + predictability outperforms binary cry/no-cry framing; parents' confidence predicts success; anxiety transfers
  Techniques: Responsive settling; predictable rituals; parental co-regulation
  Evidence: mixed-methods

## Instructions
Recommend 4-6 books that:
- Are REAL, well-known published books (do NOT invent titles)
- Cover diverse categories (mix of parenting guides, therapy workbooks, psychology, child development)
- Are directly relevant to the therapeutic goal and research findings
- Would be accessible and practical for a parent or caregiver
- Range from introductory to advanced where appropriate

goal_title: Help my autistic 9-year-old with sensory overwhelm at school
prompt:
You are a clinical bibliotherapist. Based on the therapeutic goal, family context, and academic research papers below, recommend 4-6 real, published books that would be most helpful.

## Therapeutic Goal
Goal: Help my autistic 9-year-old with sensory overwhelm at school
Description: Theo is diagnosed Level 1 autistic with sensory sensitivities (noise, fluorescents, transitions). He holds it together all day then melts down at pickup. We want a neurodiversity-affirming plan.

## Family Context
### Person Profile
**Theo** | Age: 9 | Relationship: son | Bio: Autistic, gifted verbal, loves facts about trains and space. Masks at school. Selective eater; avoids loud places.
### Known Issues (2)
- **After-school restraint collapse** [severe/behavior]: Cries, rages, or shuts down every day between 3-5 pm.
- **Masking fatigue** [moderate/emotion]: Suppresses stims and emotions during class; says school makes him 'feel like a robot'.

## Research Papers (for grounding)
[1] "Sensory processing differences in autism: neuroimaging evidence"
  Abstract: Review of 60 neuroimaging studies documenting atypical sensory gating in autistic brains.
  Key findings: Not a deficit model; differences in salience network; sensory profiles are stable and predictive of function
  Techniques: Sensory profiling; environmental accommodations; sensory diet
  Evidence: systematic review

[2] "Masking and autistic burnout in children and adolescents"
  Abstract: Community-participatory study (n=480 autistic youth).
  Key findings: Masking correlates with anxiety and burnout; unmasking at home is healthy decompression; restraint collapse is normal
  Techniques: Unmasking safe spaces; scheduled decompression; validating stims
  Evidence: participatory research

[3] "Neurodiversity-affirming therapy for autistic children"
  Abstract: Practitioner guidelines from autistic-led research consortium.
  Key findings: Pathologizing frames damage identity; strength-based support outperforms compliance training; self-advocacy is protective
  Techniques: Strength inventories; self-advocacy teaching; interest-driven engagement; affirming language
  Evidence: clinical guidelines

[4] "Interoception and emotion regulation in autistic youth"
  Abstract: Study (n=146) linking interoceptive awareness to emotion-regulation outcomes.
  Key findings: Interoceptive training improves recognition; body-based cues precede emotional labeling; linked to alexithymia
  Techniques: Body check-ins; graded interoceptive exposure; emotion-body mapping
  Evidence: correlational

## Instructions
Recommend 4-6 books that:
- Are REAL, well-known published books (do NOT invent titles)
- Cover diverse categories (mix of parenting guides, therapy workbooks, psychology, child development)
- Are directly relevant to the therapeutic goal and research findings
- Would be accessible and practical for a parent or caregiver
- Range from introductory to advanced where appropriate

For each book provide: title (exact), authors (array of full names), year (int, optional), isbn (string, optional), description (brief content summary), whyRecommended (personalized rationale linking to the goal, research, and family context), category (one of: parenting, therapy, self-help, child development, education, psychology, neuroscience).

Respond with a JSON object of the shape {"books": [ {...}, ... ]}. The top-level key MUST be "books" and its value MUST be an array.

goal_title: Support my 10-year-old through the death of her grandmother
prompt:
You are a clinical bibliotherapist. Based on the therapeutic goal, family context, and academic research papers below, recommend 4-6 real, published books that would be most helpful.

## Therapeutic Goal
Goal: Support my 10-year-old through the death of her grandmother
Description: Anya's grandmother died three months ago after a long illness. She was primary weekend caregiver. Anya alternates between looking 'fine' and sudden meltdowns about unrelated topics. We want to understand developmentally-appropriate grief and how to support her.

## Family Context
### Person Profile
**Anya** | Age: 10 | Relationship: daughter | Bio: Sensitive, artistic, very close to her grandmother. Big feelings but private about them. School performance unchanged; teachers don't notice anything.
### Known Issues (2)
- **Grief eruptions** [moderate/emotion]: Sudden crying at small frustrations; often then clams up.
- **Avoidance of grandma's things** [mild/behavior]: Won't enter the room where grandma used to read to her; won't look at photos.

## Research Papers (for grounding)
[1] "Childhood bereavement: developmental patterns and long-term outcomes"
  Abstract: 20-year cohort study (n=1,200) of bereaved children.
  Key findings: Grief comes in waves, not stages; kids grieve in bursts then return to play; unresolved grief raises adult depression risk; ongoing bonds help
  Techniques: Normalizing the pattern; dual-process model; continuing bonds; memory-making
  Evidence: longitudinal cohort

[2] "Complicated grief versus adaptive mourning in middle childhood"
  Abstract: Clinical review of when to seek specialized grief therapy.
  Key findings: Avoidance and numbing are normal short-term; concerning if persistent past 6-12 months; traumatic death has different profile
  Techniques: Monitoring indicators; graded exposure to memories; play/art-based expression
  Evidence: clinical review

[3] "Meaning-making and narrative therapy with grieving children"
  Abstract: Qualitative study of narrative-therapy processes with n=42 children aged 7-12.
  Key findings: Story-making externalizes loss; memory boxes provide containment; letters to the deceased reduce ambiguous distress
  Techniques: Memory boxes; letter writing; externalizing; shared stories
  Evidence: qualitative

## Instructions
Recommend 4-6 books that:
- Are REAL, well-known published books (do NOT invent titles)
- Cover diverse categories (mix of parenting guides, therapy workbooks, psychology, child development)
- Are directly relevant to the therapeutic goal and research findings
- Would be accessible and practical for a parent or caregiver
- Range from introductory to advanced where appropriate

For each book provide: title (exact), authors (array of full names), year (int, optional), isbn (string, optional), description (brief content summary), whyRecommended (personalized rationale linking to the goal, research, and family context), category (one of: parenting, therapy, self-help, child development, education, psychology, neuroscience).

Respond with a JSON object of the shape {"books": [ {...}, ... ]}. The top-level key MUST be "books" and its value MUST be an array.

goal_title: Recover from parenting burnout as a primary caregiver of three young kids
prompt:
You are a clinical bibliotherapist. Based on the therapeutic goal, family context, and academic research papers below, recommend 4-6 real, published books that would be most helpful.

## Therapeutic Goal
Goal: Recover from parenting burnout as a primary caregiver of three young kids
Description: I've been solo parenting three kids under 8 for two years while my partner travels. I'm numb, resentful, and losing patience. Looking for resources grounded in burnout neuroscience, not just 'self-care' platitudes.

## Family Context
### Person Profile
**Daniela** | Age: 38 | Relationship: self | Bio: Works part-time. Loves her kids but doesn't recognize herself anymore. Isolated from adult friends.
### Known Issues (2)
- **Irritability and shouting** [moderate/emotion]: Snaps at kids for small things; regrets it; cycle repeats.
- **Emotional numbness** [moderate/mood]: Going through motions; can't access joy or pride.

## Research Papers (for grounding)
[1] "Parental burnout: definition, prevalence, and consequences"
  Abstract: Cross-cultural study across 42 countries (n=17,000).
  Key findings: Distinct from depression; driven by chronic mismatch between demands and resources; associated with escape ideation and neglect/violence risk
  Techniques: Demand-resource rebalancing; validation; recognizing burnout signs; seeking structural support
  Evidence: large-scale cross-sectional

[2] "Stress cycle completion and nervous-system regulation"
  Abstract: Translational review linking polyvagal theory to practical stress recovery.
  Key findings: Chronic stress stays activated unless completed; movement, breath, connection are evidence-based completers; small and consistent > big and rare
  Techniques: Stress cycle completion; vagus nerve activation; co-regulation with peers
  Evidence: narrative review

[3] "Maternal mental load and invisible labor: impact on wellbeing"
  Abstract: Mixed-methods study (n=280 mothers) of cognitive labor distribution.
  Key findings: Anticipation + monitoring + coordination = hidden toll; naming the load reduces gaslighting; renegotiation outperforms silent suffering
  Techniques: Naming invisible labor; fair-play style reallocation; boundary-setting
  Evidence: mixed-methods

## Instructions
Recommend 4-6 books that:
- Are REAL, well-known published books (do NOT invent titles)
- Cover diverse categories (mix of parenting guides, therapy workbooks, psychology, child development)
- Are directly relevant to the therapeutic goal and research findings
- Would be accessible and practical for a parent or caregiver
- Range from introductory to advanced where appropriate

For each book provide: title (exact), authors (array of full names), year (int, optional), isbn (string, optional), description (brief content summary), whyRecommended (personalized rationale linking to the goal, research, and family context), category (one of: parenting, therapy, self-help, child development, education, psychology, neuroscience).

Respond with a JSON object of the shape {"books": [ {...}, ... ]}. The top-level key MUST be "books" and its value MUST be an array.

goal_title: Address compulsive screen and gaming use in my 13-year-old
prompt:
You are a clinical bibliotherapist. Based on the therapeutic goal, family context, and academic research papers below, recommend 4-6 real, published books that would be most helpful.

## Therapeutic Goal
Goal: Address compulsive screen and gaming use in my 13-year-old
Description: Sam spends 5-7 hours/day gaming on weekends and resists any limit. Grades slipping, sleep suffering, real-world friendships fading. Want to understand the mechanisms and apply effective family strategies without a blanket ban.

## Family Context
### Person Profile
**Sam** | Age: 13 | Relationship: son | Bio: Gaming started as social during the pandemic. Good kid but defensive about screens. Introverted; in-person friends tapered off.
### Known Issues (2)
- **Screen-limit conflict** [severe/behavior]: Rage, accusations, stonewalling when limits are attempted.
- **Sleep displacement** [moderate/sleep]: Up gaming until 1-2 am on weekends; daytime exhaustion.

## Research Papers (for grounding)
[1] "Variable-ratio reinforcement and game design in adolescent behavior"
  Abstract: Review of behavioral-economics research applied to modern game loops.
  Key findings: Loot boxes and skinner-box mechanics hook developing reward systems; dopamine depletion reduces baseline mood; not moral failing
  Techniques: Psychoeducation on mechanics; non-game dopamine activities; scheduled breaks; friction design
  Evidence: narrative review

[2] "Self-determination theory and healthy screen use in adolescents"
  Abstract: Longitudinal study (n=812) linking autonomy-competence-relatedness to moderation.
  Key findings: Autonomy-supportive parenting predicts internalized limits; controlling approaches backfire; replacing needs met by games > subtracting games
  Techniques: Curious questioning; collaborative agreements; needs-replacement planning
  Evidence: longitudinal

[3] "Sleep and adolescent digital use: a biological perspective"
  Abstract: Review of circadian effects of nighttime screen use.
  Key findings: Melatonin suppression; cognitive arousal from competitive games; rebound REM disruption; school-day functioning impaired
  Techniques: Sleep window protection; wind-down protocols; device parking
  Evidence: narrative review

[4] "Motivational interviewing with adolescents on problematic tech use"
  Abstract: RCT (n=118 adolescent-parent dyads) of MI-based parent training.
  Key findings: MI lowers reactance; reflective listening surfaces ambivalence; change-talk precedes behavior change
  Techniques: Reflective listening; double-sided reflections; eliciting change talk
  Evidence: randomized controlled trial

## Instructions
Recommend 4-6 books that:
- Are REAL, well-known published books (do NOT invent titles)
- Cover diverse categories (mix of parenting guides, therapy workbooks, psychology, child development)
- Are directly relevant to the therapeutic goal and research findings
- Would be accessible and practical for a parent or caregiver
- Range from introductory to advanced where appropriate

For each book provide: title (exact), authors (array of full names), year (int, optional), isbn (string, optional), description (brief content summary), whyRecommended (personalized rationale linking to the goal, research, and family context), category (one of: parenting, therapy, self-help, child development, education, psychology, neuroscience).

Respond with a JSON object of the shape {"books": [ {...}, ... ]}. The top-level key MUST be "books" and its value MUST be an array.
